literal: enumeration, reckoning; from saṅkhyā (number) — the school that enumerates the principles (tattvas)
invariant_definition: Sāṅkhya is one of the oldest of the six orthodox darśanas, classically systematized in Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṅkhya-Kārikā (c. 4th c. CE). It is a dualist enumeration of reality into two ultimate, irreducible principles: puruṣa, the plural, inactive, pure consciousness, and prakṛti, the single, unconscious, active material nature constituted of three strands (guṇas: sattva, rajas, tamas). The manifest world evolves from prakṛti through a determinate sequence of twenty-three further principles (tattvas). Bondage is the non-discrimination of puruṣa from prakṛti; liberation (kaivalya) is the discriminative knowledge (viveka) that isolates consciousness from nature. Classical Sāṅkhya is non-theistic and supplies the metaphysics for which Yoga is the practical complement.
